one_line_takeaway: The paper formalizes and empirically validates 'Salami Slicing Risk' -- a per-turn refusal mechanism can be defeated by chaining many individually sub-threshold prompts that accumulate harmful intent -- and shows the resulting Salami Attack achieves >90% ASR while transferring across models/modalities essentially for free and resisting existing defenses, motivating their cumulative-history-auditing (CQA) countermeasure.
key_finding: Salami Attack (5-shot and 10-shot) and its adaptive variant A-Salami outperform all seven baselines across all 15 model-dataset combinations in Table II. Representative numbers (ASR%, Harmful Score in parentheses) on AdvBench: GPT-4o 86.9(8.47)/90.4(9.19) for Salami 5/10-shot and 95.8(9.37) for A-Salami vs. 58.3(5.76) Crescendo, 65.3(6.01) GOAT, 51.7(4.98) CIA, 30.6(3.82) CFA, 39.8(4.39) PAIR; on Gemini 2.5 Pro: 91.2(9.06)/91.5(9.11)/94.6(9.28) for Salami/A-Salami vs. best baseline GOAT 70.5(6.97). A-Salami reaches >=95.0% ASR in 10 of 15 model-dataset pairs. Efficiency (Table III, AdvBench, GPT-4o): Salami AVQ 3.9 vs. Crescendo 5.6 vs. PAIR 10.7; AVT 728.91 tokens vs. Crescendo 5013.49 vs. PAIR 6221.29 (paper reports >80% token-cost reduction and ~50-60% faster execution). Transferability (Table IV, JailbreakBench, GPT-4o-optimized prompts moved to other models): Salami(5-shot) shows zero ASR drop (91.0/93.0/93.0 on Gemini/GLM-4.5/Qwen3) while A-Salami drops 7.0-13.0 points and Crescendo drops 21.0-24.0 points. Multimodal (Table V/VI): VLM ASR 55.56% on GPT-4o and 56.15% on Qwen-VL-Max vs. best baseline SI-Attack 52.17%/55.15%; diffusion Bypass Rate 16/20 (80%) with 13/16 (81%) Consistency Rate vs. SneakyPrompt 13/20 bypass, 10/13 consistent. Defense robustness (Table VIII, JailbreakBench, GPT-4o): Salami(5-shot) average ASR 83.5% across SmoothLLM/Self-Reminder/Input-Filter defenses (only a 4.0% relative drop from the undefended 87.0%), versus Crescendo's 54.3% average (15.2% drop) and PAIR's 16.0% average (46.7% drop). CQA defense (Table IX, JailbreakBench, GPT-4o): reduces ASR by 44.8% for Salami(5-shot) (87.0->48.0), 28.6% for A-Salami (91.0->65.0), 40.6% for Crescendo (64.0->38.0), and 64.8% for CIA (54.0->19.0), with zero over-refusals on 52 benign Alpaca prompts.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is threefold: (1) 'Salami Slicing Risk', a formalized theoretical mechanism (Notation III.1, Definition III.1, Theorem III.1, and non-adaptive Corollary III.1) showing that because alignment/refusal is modeled as a per-turn threshold check on incremental harm H̃(p_{n+1}, p_n), an attacker can chain a sequence of prompts each below the refusal threshold τ_thres to make cumulative conversational harm H(p*) exceed any target harm level h, without any single explicit malicious trigger turn; (2) the 'Salami Attack' framework built on this risk -- a manual variant, a fully automated variant (Algorithm 1: an attack LLM decomposes a harmful prompt p into an initial safe prompt p0 plus k progressive perturbations each repeated t times), a multi-modal extension (VLMs and text-to-image diffusion models), and a 'Multi-Agent Adaptive Salami Attack' (A-Salami) using four cooperating agents (Reflection Thinker, Attacker, Evaluation Agents, Summary Agent) for closed-loop adaptive refinement; and (3) 'Cumulative Query Auditing' (CQA), a proposed defense that re-feeds the full concatenated prompt history to the aligned model itself at every turn so it judges cumulative rather than per-turn harm, with a formal argument (Assumption VI.1, Lemma VI.1, Theorem VI.1) that CQA can, under stated assumptions, provably intercept any Salami-style attack before harm exceeds τ_thres.
threat_model: Black-box threat model (explicitly stated, Section III-A): the attacker treats the target LLM/VLM/diffusion model purely as a black box with no knowledge of internals, requiring only (a) an attack-generation LLM capable of producing prompts satisfying the risk's decomposition requirements (paper uses GPT-4) and (b) API/interface access to the target model. Attacker background knowledge requirement is stated as minimal ('basic awareness of salami slicing risks suffices'). Attacker's goal is to elicit harmful text, images, or other modality outputs aligned with a specific malicious intent via multi-turn interactions that never individually exceed the per-turn refusal threshold.
limitations: The paper's own stated/implicit limitations: (1) the core theoretical framework (Theorem III.1, Assumption III.1) treats alignment as a simplified per-turn refusal-threshold mechanism and Assumption VI.1 (Aggregate Harm Equivalence) is explicitly called an 'idealized' approximation that 'real-world deviations (e.g., context drift, model-specific processing quirks) may weaken'; (2) due to policy constraints the authors could not red-team OpenAI's o1/o3 or the Claude model series (footnote 3), so the strongest claimed frontier-model results exclude those families; (3) failure-case analysis attributes most residual failures to dataset bias (inherently non-harmful items in benchmarks) rather than defense success, with contextual drift and attacker-model refusal as secondary factors; (4) CQA is explicitly presented as a prototype ('we leave further optimization of the CQA prototype as future work') that still leaves substantial residual ASR (e.g., 48.0% and 65.0% for Salami/A-Salami even after defense) rather than fully neutralizing the attack; (5) CQA's self-defense mechanism relies on using the same aligned model as its own judge over concatenated history, which the authors do not stress-test against adaptive attackers aware of CQA; (6) time measurements are noted by the authors themselves as confounded by network latency and infrastructure variance and are only comparable within their own controlled setup, not as absolute figures.

datasets_used: HarmBench (200 standard textual jailbreak prompts, 6 harm categories)
MM-SafetyBench (5,040 text-image pairs, 13 high-risk scenarios; 10% sampled)
OVERT (1,785 harmful/unsafe prompts for text-to-image models; 20 sampled)
Stanford Alpaca (52 benign prompts sampled, used for CQA over-refusal check)
quantitative_results: Salami Attack ASR ~90% on GPT-4o and Gemini across benchmarks (abstract headline figure)
AdvBench GPT-4o: Salami 5-shot 86.9% ASR (8.47 harm score), 10-shot 90.4% (9.19), A-Salami 95.8% (9.37) vs. best baseline GOAT 65.3% (6.01)
AdvBench Gemini 2.5 Pro: Salami 5/10-shot 91.2/91.5% ASR vs. best baseline GOAT 70.5%
Efficiency on AdvBench/GPT-4o: AVQ 3.9 (Salami) vs 5.6 (Crescendo) vs 10.7 (PAIR); AVT 728.91 vs 5013.49 vs 6221.29 tokens; execution time 88.35s vs 168.83s vs 202.75s
Transferability (GPT-4o-optimized, JailbreakBench): Salami(5-shot) 91.0/93.0/93.0% ASR on Gemini/GLM-4.5/Qwen3 with 0-point drop vs. Crescendo drops of 24.0/21.0/13.0 points
VLM ASR: Salami 55.56% (GPT-4o), 56.15% (Qwen-VL-Max) vs. best baseline SI-Attack 52.17%/55.15%
Diffusion (Gemini web interface): Salami Bypass Rate 16/20 (80%), Consistency 13/16 (81%) vs. SneakyPrompt 13/20 bypass, 10/13 consistency; vs. DACA 9/20 bypass, 5/9 consistency
Defense robustness (JailbreakBench/GPT-4o, avg ASR across SmoothLLM/Self-Reminder/Input-Filter): Salami(5-shot) 83.5% (-4.0% relative) vs. Crescendo 54.3% (-15.2%) vs. PAIR 16.0% (-46.7%)

CQA defense (JailbreakBench/GPT-4o): ASR reduction of 44.8% for Salami(5-shot, 87.0->48.0), 28.6% for A-Salami (91.0->65.0), 40.6% for Crescendo (64.0->38.0), maximum 64.8% for CIA (54.0->19.0); zero false refusals on 52 benign Alpaca prompts
Mechanistic validation: 96% of 50 sampled 10-shot Llama-2-7B-Chat Salami attacks show peak safety-attention (L2H26) on the final attack turn
baselines_compared: Crescendo (Russinovich et al., multi-turn adaptive attack, PyRIT implementation)
GOAT (Generative Offensive Agent Tester, multi-agent automated jailbreak)
PAIR (Prompt Automatic Iterative Refinement, EasyJailbreak implementation)
GCG and MSJ (listed in qualitative Table I comparison only, not in the main quantitative benchmarks)
SneakyPrompt and DACA (text-to-image / diffusion jailbreak baselines)
SmoothLLM, Self-Reminder, and LLM-based Input Filter (defense baselines evaluated against, not attack baselines)
